# Compat sections

fixtures.txt holds the STRING, BITMAP, HLL, HASH, and SET manifest
rows from spec doc 12 section 1, generated by gen.py against a real
redis-server 8.8.0 and replayed byte for byte by
TestCompatRedisParity through the sqlo1 dispatch path.

Regenerate with:

    redis-server --port 7399 --save '' --appendonly no --daemonize no &
    python3 gen.py 7399 > fixtures.txt

## Where each manifest row is diffed

| Doc 12 row | Coverage |
|------------|----------|
| GET, SET (all flags), SETNX, SETEX, PSETEX, GETDEL, GETEX, STRLEN, SUBSTR | STRING section here |
| APPEND, GETRANGE, SETRANGE | STRING section here |
| INCR, DECR, INCRBY, DECRBY, INCRBYFLOAT | STRING section here (see the float note below) |
| MGET, MSET, MSETNX | STRING section here |
| LCS | canonical rows here; the full option matrix is pinned live in lcs_test.go |
| SETBIT, GETBIT, BITFIELD, BITFIELD_RO | BITMAP section here |
| BITCOUNT, BITPOS | BITMAP section here |
| BITOP | BITMAP section here |
| PFADD, PFCOUNT, PFMERGE, PFDEBUG, PFSELFTEST | surface rows here; deep parity (envelope bytes, estimator, debug views, promotion) in testdata/hll |
| HSET, HSETNX, HMSET, HGET, HMGET, HDEL, HEXISTS, HSTRLEN, HLEN | HASH section here |
| HINCRBY, HINCRBYFLOAT | HASH section here (same float note as INCRBYFLOAT) |
| HGETALL, HKEYS, HVALS, HSCAN | HASH section here (listpack-tier order; segmented-tier order is engine-defined and pinned in hashiter tests) |
| HRANDFIELD | deterministic rows here (misses, count 0, one-field forcing); distribution lives in the hrand lab |
| HGETEX, HGETDEL | HASH section here |
| HEXPIRE, HPEXPIRE, HEXPIREAT, HPEXPIREAT, HTTL, HPTTL, HEXPIRETIME, HPEXPIRETIME, HPERSIST | HASH section here (condition table over absolute stamps, past-time deletes, key death, the full error grammar) |
| SADD, SREM, SISMEMBER, SMISMEMBER, SCARD, SMOVE | SET section here |
| SMEMBERS, SSCAN | SET section here (listpack-tier insertion order and ascending intsets, where both sides agree; see the order note below) |
| SPOP, SRANDMEMBER | deterministic rows here (misses, count 0, one-member forcing, overpop); distribution lives in the spop lab |
| SINTER, SUNION, SDIFF, SINTERCARD | integer replies, single-member results, and absent-key rows here; wider results pinned through the STORE variants and SMISMEMBER probes (order note below) |
| SINTERSTORE, SUNIONSTORE, SDIFFSTORE | SET section here (integer replies, empty-result key delete, TTL drop, type overwrite) |

TYPE, TTL, OBJECT ENCODING, and DEL rows appear as scaffolding where a
string behavior needs them (TTL discard and KEEPTTL rules, encoding
boundaries); their own families are keyspace milestones.

## Divergences found and fixed while pinning

- SSCAN with a NOVALUES token answers a dedicated error in Redis 8.8
  ("NOVALUES option can only be used in HSCAN"), because the scan
  option grammar is shared across the scan family and the option is
  gated after parsing; sqlo1 fell through to the generic syntax
  error. Fixed in sscanCmd.

- GETRANGE with a still-negative end after adding the length clamps to
  byte 0 in Redis (GETRANGE v -100 -50 is the first byte), and the
  same rule holds for BITCOUNT and BITPOS ranges; sqlo1's shared clamp
  returned an empty window. Fixed in clampRange.
- OBJECT ENCODING on a missing key is a null bulk in Redis 8.8, not
  the "no such key" error older releases sent. Fixed in server.go.
- The HEXPIRE set family's grammar errors moved between releases: 8.8
  answers "unknown argument: X" for a bad condition token or a
  misplaced FIELDS after a valid condition, and a plain "wrong number
  of arguments" when numfields overshoots the args, while the read
  family (HTTL and friends) kept the older Mandatory-FIELDS and
  numfields-match texts. sqlo1 had implemented the t_hash.c 8.0 texts
  for both families. Fixed in hexpireCmd/hfeFields.
- An inline hash holding a field TTL answers listpackex to OBJECT
  ENCODING, not listpack. Fixed in Hash.Encoding.

## Documented divergences that stand

- OBJECT ENCODING reports rope for values over the rope boundary,
  where Redis says raw. The representation genuinely differs and
  hiding it would cost an existence check on every encoding probe.
- The hash encoding count boundary differs: sqlo1 goes segmented
  (hashtable) past 128 fields or 2 KiB inline bytes, Redis 8.8's
  defaults are 512 entries and 64 B values. The fixture crosses the
  boundary through the value-size wall, which both sides share in
  spirit, and does not assert encoding in the 129-511 field window.
- Redis keeps listpackex once a field TTL has existed, even after the
  last TTL is persisted; sqlo1 reports listpack again the moment no
  field carries one, since the inline region genuinely holds no TTL
  bytes then.
- Set replies are unordered by contract and the orders genuinely
  differ where the representations do: Redis walks a numerically
  sorted intset or a hashtable, sqlo1 keeps insertion order inline
  and emits in fh order once segmented, algebra included. The
  fixture only pins multi-member arrays where the orders provably
  agree (listpack insertion order, intsets inserted ascending) and
  covers everything wider through SCARD, SINTERCARD, the STORE
  variants' integer replies, and SMISMEMBER probes.
- The set encoding count boundaries differ the same way the hash's
  do: sqlo1 segments (hashtable) past 128 members or 2 KiB inline
  bytes, Redis 8.8's defaults are 512 for both set-max-intset-entries
  and set-max-listpack-entries. The fixture crosses through the
  member-size wall (members over 64 B leave Redis's listpack, and 30
  of them blow sqlo1's byte cap) and does not assert encoding in the
  129-511 member window.
- INCRBYFLOAT arithmetic runs at float64. Redis computes in long
  double, and redis builds disagree with each other here: on macOS
  long double is 64-bit and 10.5 + 0.1 prints 10.59999999999999964,
  while the Linux gate rivals compute in 80-bit x87 and print 10.6,
  which is also what sqlo1 prints (verified against the frozen 8.8.0
  on the gate box). Values needing more than 53 mantissa bits stay
  different: Linux Redis answers 4.00000000000000001 to
  3 + 1.000000000000000005 where sqlo1 answers 4. Go has no long
  double; the fixture keeps to exactly-representable increments and
  the suite treats the tail digits as out of scope.
